content: Ho Chi Minh declared Vietnamese independence from France, citing the American Declaration of Independence
context: After Japan's surrender in WW2, Ho Chi Minh proclaimed the Democratic Republic of Vietnam on September 2, 1945. He quoted Jefferson: 'All men are created equal.' The US chose to support France instead.

content: France defeated at Dien Bien Phu — end of French colonial rule in Indochina
context: General Giap besieged 16,000 French troops using artillery dragged through mountains. The 57-day siege ended French will to fight. The Geneva Accords divided Vietnam at the 17th parallel.

content: Domino Theory — US policymakers believed that if one Southeast Asian country fell to communism, others would follow
context: Eisenhower articulated the theory in 1954. It became the intellectual foundation for US involvement in Vietnam. The logic: lose Vietnam, lose Laos, Cambodia, Thailand, Burma, and eventually all of Asia.

content: US backed Ngo Dinh Diem as leader of South Vietnam despite his authoritarian and corrupt rule
context: Diem cancelled the 1956 reunification elections (which Ho Chi Minh would have won). His Catholic-favoring policies alienated the Buddhist majority. US support continued until his assassination in 1963.

content: Ho Chi Minh Trail established as the primary supply route from North Vietnam through Laos and Cambodia to the South
context: The trail was a network of paths, roads, and river routes spanning 1,000+ miles. Despite massive US bombing (Operation Rolling Thunder dropped more bombs than all of WW2), the trail was never cut.

content: Gulf of Tonkin incident — alleged North Vietnamese attack on US destroyers led to Congressional war authorization
context: The first attack (August 2) was real but minor. The second attack (August 4) likely never happened. Johnson used it to secure the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution, giving him blank-check war authority.

content: Operation Rolling Thunder — sustained US bombing campaign against North Vietnam failed to break Hanoi's will
context: From 1965-1968, the US dropped 864,000 tons of bombs on North Vietnam. The campaign was gradually escalated with pauses for diplomacy. It failed because North Vietnam's economy was primarily agrarian.

content: US ground troop deployment escalated from 23,000 advisors to 536,000 combat troops by 1968
context: General Westmoreland's 'attrition strategy' used search-and-destroy missions and body counts as metrics. The draft fueled domestic opposition. The US military was designed for conventional war, not counterinsurgency.

content: Viet Cong guerrilla warfare used tunnel networks, booby traps, and hit-and-run tactics against superior US firepower
context: The Cu Chi tunnels stretched 250 km near Saigon. The Viet Cong controlled the tempo of engagement — they chose when to fight and when to disappear. US technological superiority was neutralized by jungle terrain.

content: Tet Offensive — coordinated North Vietnamese/Viet Cong attack on 100+ cities shocked the American public
context: Launched during the Tet holiday ceasefire on January 30, 1968. Militarily, it was a devastating defeat for the VC (45,000 killed). Politically, it was a strategic victory — Americans saw the war was not being won.

content: Walter Cronkite declared the war a 'stalemate' on national television, turning mainstream American opinion against the war
context: After visiting Vietnam during Tet, the 'most trusted man in America' said: 'We are mired in stalemate.' Johnson reportedly said: 'If I've lost Cronkite, I've lost Middle America.'

content: Nixon implemented 'Vietnamization' — gradually withdrawing US troops while building up South Vietnamese forces
context: Nixon reduced US troop levels from 536,000 to 24,000 between 1969-1972. The strategy assumed South Vietnam could defend itself with US air support and equipment. It could not.

content: Paris Peace Accords signed — US withdrew all forces in exchange for POW return and ceasefire
context: Kissinger and Le Duc Tho negotiated for years. The accords allowed North Vietnamese troops to remain in the South. Everyone knew the ceasefire would not hold. Kissinger and Tho shared the Nobel Peace Prize.

content: Fall of Saigon — North Vietnamese tanks crashed through the gates of the Presidential Palace, reunifying Vietnam
context: On April 30, 1975, Saigon fell. Chaotic helicopter evacuations from the US Embassy became the defining image of American defeat. 58,220 Americans, 250,000 South Vietnamese soldiers, and 1.1 million NVA/VC died.

content: Vietnam War killed an estimated 3.1 million Vietnamese (including 2 million civilians) and 58,220 Americans
context: Agent Orange defoliated 5 million acres and caused birth defects for decades. 7 million tons of bombs were dropped — more than all of WW2 combined. The war cost the US $168 billion ($1 trillion adjusted).

content: My Lai Massacre revealed — US soldiers killed 347-504 unarmed Vietnamese civilians, fueling anti-war movement
context: On March 16, 1968, Charlie Company murdered villagers in My Lai. The coverup lasted over a year. Only Lt. William Calley was convicted. The massacre became a symbol of the war's moral corruption.
